title: Feature-aware Modulation for Learning from Temporal Tabular Data
authors: Hao-Run Cai, Han-Jia Ye (Nanjing University, LAMDA)
tokenization_method: Feature-aware temporal modulation: a lightweight FiLM-style hypernetwork (the 'modulator') consumes a shared temporal embedding psi(t) (Fourier periodic priors over year/month/day/hour + a trend term, fixed dim 128) and emits per-feature-dimension parameters (gamma=scale, beta=bias, lambda=Yeo-Johnson power coefficient). Each feature is reparameterized as x_tilde_i = gamma_i(psi(t)) * YJ(x_i; lambda_i(psi(t))) + beta_i(psi(t)), where YJ is the Yeo-Johnson power transform. This time-conditioned affine+nonlinear (power) transform aligns the mean/std/skewness of each numerical feature across temporal stages so that 'subjective' feature semantics (e.g. what counts as 'high income') stay consistent over time. Modulation can be inserted at three sites: raw input ('In'), intermediate representation ('Rep'), and output logits ('Out'); full modulation applies all three with weight-sharing per block.
feature_types_handled: Numerical (primary target of the modulation, via mean/std/skewness statistics) + temporal (timestamp t is the conditioning variable, not a modulated feature). Categorical features are passed through the backbone's standard embeddings and are NOT the focus of the modulation. Mixed numerical+categorical industrial tables (TabReD) overall; no explicit missing-value mechanism described.
token_granularity: Per-feature-dimension modulation parameters: the modulator outputs gamma/beta/lambda vectors in R^m (m = feature dimension), so each scalar feature gets its own time-conditioned transform. This is a feature-wise reparameterization upstream of / interleaved with the tokenizer, not a discrete token vocabulary.
numerical_strategy: Time-conditioned Yeo-Johnson power transform (handles skewed & heavy-tailed, signed values) followed by an affine scale+shift, all parameterized by a hypernetwork over the temporal embedding. Explicitly an alternative/complement to fixed numerical embeddings: it modulates distributional statistics (bias=mean, scale=std, shape=skewness) rather than learning fixed bins or PLR features.
categorical_strategy: Not addressed by the modulation; relies on the backbone (MLP / MLP-PLR / TabM) default categorical embeddings.
temporal_handling: Timestamp is mapped to a temporal embedding psi(t) (reused from Cai & Ye 2025) combining Fourier periodic priors (year/month/day/hour orders) and a trend component, fixed embedding dim 128. Crucially the temporal modality is decoupled from the input modality: psi(t) drives the modulator but is not concatenated to features, which the paper claims avoids the dimension-scaling degradation seen when temporal embeddings are concatenated to inputs.
scales_to_many_features: Modulator output is O(3*m) parameters (gamma/beta/lambda per feature dim), far cheaper than a full weight-generating hypernetwork; the temporal embedding is shared across all instances and modulators for parameter efficiency. Evaluated on the TabReD benchmark (8 large industrial tabular datasets with dozens-to-hundreds of heterogeneous numerical+categorical columns), so it operates in the many-feature regime, though the paper does not report a feature-count ablation or per-feature compute scaling curve.
downstream_model: MLP (full 3-site modulation), MLP-PLR and TabM (single raw-input modulation only). Backbone-agnostic: described as a drop-in input layer requiring no change to backbone internals.
pretraining_objective: None. Standard supervised ERM end-to-end (cross-entropy for classification AUC tasks, MSE for regression RMSE tasks); the modulator is trained jointly with the backbone.
key_results: On TabReD (refined Cai & Ye 2025 protocol, 15 seeds): TabM + temporal modulation reaches the best average rank 3.50 across 8 datasets, beating all static baselines (CatBoost rank 4.375, XGBoost 6.375) and all temporal-embedding adaptive baselines. Plain MLP + full modulation improves +2.09% (relative) over plain MLP and outperforms most deep methods. Authors claim this is the first time a deep tabular method consistently outperforms GBDTs under temporal shift. Ablation (Table 2): raw-input modulation alone gives 87.4% of the full gain; removing raw-input modulation drops gain to 56.8%, so early-stage modulation is the dominant contributor.
baselines_compared: Static: Linear, XGBoost, CatBoost, LightGBM, RandomForest, MLP, MLP-PLR, FT-Transformer, TabR, ModernNCA, TabM. Adaptive: the same six deep models with concatenated temporal embedding (Cai & Ye 2025). Conceptual contrast: FiLM (linear-only modulation), full weight-generating hypernetworks (too costly), Drift-Resilient TabPFN.
transferable_idea: Time-conditioned, per-feature affine+power (Yeo-Johnson) reparameterization driven by a shared temporal embedding hypernetwork (FiLM-for-tables): a cheap, drop-in input-level transform that aligns each feature's distributional statistics across time before tokenization. Directly applicable to the time-varying half of a 70+-feature mixed set, and the decoupling of temporal modality from input modality is a reusable design pattern for temporal-static fusion.
limitations: Stated: full-stage modulation is incompatible with PLR embeddings (PLR maps numericals to sine/cosine -> arcsine distribution, which breaks the semantic mean/std/skew assumption); for PLR/TabM only single raw-input modulation is usable. Implied: modulation targets numerical distributional statistics, so categorical/high-cardinality fields get no benefit; needs a meaningful timestamp per row; no missing-value handling described; gains on strong backbones (TabM +0.56%, MLP-PLR +0.18%) are far smaller than on plain MLP (+2.09%).
pitfalls: Headline 'first deep method to beat GBDTs under temporal shift' rests on average-rank over 8 TabReD datasets with small absolute AUC/RMSE deltas (e.g. TabM-mod 0.9641 vs CatBoost 0.9639 on HI) and 15-seed std overlap on several columns — the margin is thin and benchmark-specific. The big +2.09% is on a weak MLP backbone; on SOTA backbones the gain mostly vanishes, and on those the PLR incompatibility forces the weaker single-site variant. This is a feature-modulation / preprocessing layer, NOT a tokenizer that creates discrete tokens or handles high-cardinality categoricals; do not over-claim it as a tokenization scheme.
relevance_to_topic: For the 70+-mixed-feature, partly-time-varying setting it supplies a concrete, cheap mechanism to keep numerical-feature semantics stable across time (temporal-static fusion + numerical-embedding layers) — a transferable input-stage modulation rather than a new token vocabulary.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/LAMDA-Tabular/Tabular-Temporal-Modulation (full implementation, config/ folder with search spaces; built on the Cai & Ye 2025 TabReD pipeline, Python 3.10 / PyTorch 2.0.1).
